children.name: Coracina
children.description: The larger cuckooshrikes — a widespread genus of medium to large passerines found from Africa through Asia to Australasia, with grey, black, and white plumage and stout, slightly hooked bills. Despite their name, they are neither cuckoos nor shrikes, but an independent family of arboreal insectivores. Many species are highly vocal with loud, fluty, far-carrying calls.

children.name: Coracina novaehollandiae
children.description: A large, grey cuckooshrike with a prominent black face mask and a white belly, found across Australia, Indonesia, and New Guinea. It is a conspicuous bird of open woodlands, often seen perched on exposed branches or powerlines, from which it sallies to catch insects in flight. Its descending, trilling call is a common sound in the Australian bush.

children.name: Coracina papuensis
children.description: A smaller, paler cuckooshrike with a white belly, a reduced black face mask, and a distinctive white rump visible in flight, ranging from eastern Australia to the Solomon Islands. It forages methodically in the canopy for insects, often joining mixed-species flocks. Its call is a thin, high-pitched whistle.

children.name: Coracina macei
children.description: A large, grey cuckooshrike with a black eye-stripe and a pale belly, found across the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, and southern China. It inhabits forests, woodlands, and well-wooded gardens, where it hunts insects from prominent perches. Its loud, fluty, three-noted whistle is a characteristic sound of Asian woodlands.

children.name: Coracina bicolor
children.description: A striking black-and-white cuckooshrike endemic to Sulawesi and adjacent islands, where it inhabits lowland and montane forests. It has a black head, upperparts, and breast, contrasting sharply with its white belly and vent. Its call is a series of clear, whistled notes.

children.name: Coracina striata
children.description: A medium-sized cuckooshrike with heavily barred underparts, found across Southeast Asia, the Philippines, and the Greater Sundas. It inhabits evergreen forests, forest edges, and mangroves. It forages in the canopy, often in pairs or small groups.

children.name: Coracina larvata
children.description: A grey cuckooshrike with a distinctive black mask and a pale throat, endemic to the mountains of Sumatra, Borneo, and Java. It inhabits montane forests, often foraging in the canopy for insects. Its call is a thin, sibilant whistle.

children.name: Coracina caeruleogrisea
children.description: A large, stout-billed cuckooshrike with blue-grey upperparts and pale grey underparts, found in the montane forests of New Guinea. It is a robust bird with a noticeably heavy bill used for taking larger insects. Its call is a loud, rasping whistle.

children.name: Coracina longicauda
children.description: A medium-sized cuckooshrike with a black hood, grey upperparts, and pale underparts, found in the montane forests of New Guinea. It has a relatively long tail compared to other Coracina species. It forages for insects in the canopy.

children.name: Coracina lineata
children.description: A medium-sized cuckooshrike with a distinctive yellow eye, grey upperparts, and finely barred underparts, found from Sulawesi to the Solomon Islands and northern Australia. It inhabits lowland forests and mangroves, often in small flocks.

children.name: Coracina boyeri
children.description: A small, grey cuckooshrike with a black face mask and pale underparts, found in the lowland forests of New Guinea and the Aru Islands. It forages actively in the canopy for insects. Its call is a thin, high-pitched whistle.

children.name: Coracina schistacea
children.description: A slaty-grey cuckooshrike with a black face mask and white underparts, endemic to the Sula Islands of the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland forests and forages for insects in the canopy. It is restricted to the islands of Taliabu, Mangole, and Sanana.

children.name: Coracina leucopygia
children.description: A striking cuckooshrike with a white rump, black upperparts, and white underparts, endemic to the island of Sulawesi. It inhabits lowland forests and forages in the canopy. Its white rump is conspicuous in flight.

children.name: Coracina atriceps
children.description: A cuckooshrike with a black head, grey upperparts, and white underparts, endemic to the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland forests and forages in the canopy. Its black cap contrasts sharply with its pale body.

children.name: Coracina caesia
children.description: A medium-sized, all-grey cuckooshrike with a pale belly and a dark eye, found in the montane forests of eastern and southern Africa. It is a quiet, inconspicuous bird that forages slowly in the forest canopy.

children.name: Coracina cinerea
children.description: A grey cuckooshrike with darker wings and tail, endemic to Madagascar and the Comoros Islands. The male is blue-grey above with a black face mask, while the female is brownish. It inhabits forests and woodlands across the island.

children.name: Coracina coerulescens
children.description: A dark, blackish-grey cuckooshrike with a pale belly, endemic to the island of Bioko and the highlands of Cameroon and Nigeria. It inhabits montane forests and is classified as Endangered due to habitat loss.

children.name: Coracina ostenta
children.description: A striking cuckooshrike with white shoulders, a black head and back, and a white belly, endemic to the islands of Panay and Negros in the Philippines. It is classified as Vulnerable due to deforestation.

children.name: Coracina personata
children.description: A medium-sized cuckooshrike with a black face mask, grey upperparts, and white underparts, found in the Moluccas and the Lesser Sundas. It inhabits lowland forests and mangroves, often in pairs.

children.name: Coracina fortis
children.description: A medium-sized cuckooshrike with blue-grey upperparts and white underparts, endemic to the island of Buru in the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland forests and forages in the canopy.

children.name: Coracina temminckii
children.description: A beautiful blue-grey cuckooshrike with a black face mask and white underparts, found in the montane forests of Sulawesi. It was named after Coenraad Jacob Temminck.

children.name: Coracina dobsoni
children.description: A grey cuckooshrike with a black face mask and pale underparts, found in the lowland forests of the Solomon Islands. It was named after naturalist George Edward Dobson.

children.name: Coracina schisticeps
children.description: A medium-sized cuckooshrike with a grey head, dark upperparts, and white underparts, found in the lowland forests of New Guinea. It forages in the canopy and is often seen in pairs.

children.name: Coracina welchmani
children.description: Welchman's Cuckooshrike is a medium-sized cuckooshrike with grey upperparts and white underparts, found in the Solomon Islands. It inhabits lowland and hill forests.

children.name: Coracina montana
children.description: The Mountain Cuckooshrike is a grey cuckooshrike found in the montane forests of New Guinea. It has dark grey upperparts and a pale belly.

children.name: Celebesica
children.description: A monotypic genus of cuckooshrike endemic to the island of Sulawesi. The single species has a distinctive crest and was formerly placed in Coracina before being moved to its own genus.

children.name: Celebesica abbotti
children.description: A medium-sized cuckooshrike with a prominent crest, grey upperparts, and white underparts, endemic to the island of Sulawesi. It inhabits lowland and montane forests, often seen in pairs. Its call is a loud, fluty whistle.

children.name: Ceblepyris
children.description: A genus of grey cuckooshrikes found in sub-Saharan Africa and Madagascar. They have relatively plain grey plumage and are often inconspicuous in the forest canopy.

children.name: Ceblepyris pectoralis
children.description: A grey cuckooshrike with a white breast and belly, found in the woodlands of eastern and southern Africa. It inhabits miombo woodlands and savanna, often in pairs or small groups. Its call is a series of thin whistles.

children.name: Ceblepyris cinereifrons
children.description: A small, grey cuckooshrike with a pale grey forehead, endemic to the island of Mindanao in the Philippines. It inhabits lowland and montane forests and is classified as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Ceblepyris graueri
children.description: A medium-sized grey cuckooshrike with pale underparts, found in the montane forests of the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Lobotos
children.description: The wattled cuckooshrikes — a distinctive genus of African cuckooshrikes with brightly coloured facial wattles. They are among the most colourful members of the Campephagidae, with males showing striking orange, yellow, or red bare skin around the eyes.

children.name: Lobotos lobatus
children.description: A striking cuckooshrike with a black head, grey body, and bright orange facial wattles, found in the lowland rainforests of West Africa. It is an active forager that gleans insects from foliage. It is classified as Near Threatened due to deforestation.

children.name: Lobotos oriolinus
children.description: A striking cuckooshrike with a black head, golden-yellow underparts, and yellow facial wattles, found in the lowland forests of Central Africa. It resembles an oriole in colouration, giving it its scientific name. It forages in the canopy for insects.

children.name: Pteropodocys
children.description: A monotypic genus of large, terrestrial cuckooshrike found across Australia and New Guinea. Unlike other cuckooshrikes, it forages primarily on the ground, running swiftly with a distinctive gait. Its elongated legs and terrestrial habits make it unique among the Campephagidae.

children.name: Pteropodocys maxima
children.description: A large, pale cuckooshrike with grey upperparts, white underparts, and a black eye-stripe, found across northern and eastern Australia and New Guinea. Unlike most cuckooshrikes, it spends much of its time on the ground, where it runs swiftly after insects. Its call is a loud, descending whistle.

children.name: Campochaera
children.description: A genus of brightly coloured cuckooshrikes with striking golden-yellow or orange underparts, restricted to New Guinea. They are among the most colourful members of the Campephagidae and inhabit lowland rainforests where they forage in the canopy.

children.name: Campochaera sloetii
children.description: A brilliantly coloured cuckooshrike with golden-yellow underparts, a black throat, and grey upperparts, endemic to the lowland forests of New Guinea. It forages in the canopy for insects and fruit, often in pairs or small groups. Its call is a thin, high-pitched whistle.

children.name: Cyanograucalus
children.description: A monotypic genus of blue-plumaged cuckooshrike found in the rainforests of West and Central Africa. The single species is notable for its brilliant azure-blue upperparts, which are unique among the otherwise grey, black, and white cuckooshrikes.

children.name: Cyanograucalus azureus
children.description: A stunning cuckooshrike with brilliant azure-blue upperparts, a black face mask, and white underparts, found in the lowland rainforests of West and Central Africa. It is the only blue-plumaged cuckooshrike in Africa and is often seen in pairs or small groups foraging in the canopy.

children.name: Edolisoma
children.description: The cicadabirds — a genus of cuckooshrikes named for their cicada-like songs. They are found from Southeast Asia through New Guinea to the southwest Pacific, with mostly grey or black-and-white plumage. Many species show striking sexual dimorphism.

children.name: Edolisoma dispar
children.description: A small, grey cicadabird endemic to the Kai Islands of Indonesia, with the male dark grey above and pale below. It inhabits lowland forests and has a distinctive cicada-like song.

children.name: Edolisoma ceramense
children.description: A pale grey cicadabird with white underparts, endemic to the island of Seram in the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland forests and forages in the canopy.

children.name: Edolisoma schisticeps
children.description: A medium-sized cicadabird with the male slaty-grey above and the female barred below, found in New Guinea and surrounding islands. It inhabits lowland and hill forests.

children.name: Edolisoma tenuirostre
children.description: A widespread cicadabird with the male dark grey and the female heavily barred, found from the Lesser Sundas to the Solomon Islands and northern Australia. Its distinctive cicada-like song is a common sound of lowland forests.

children.name: Edolisoma morio
children.description: A dark grey cicadabird endemic to Sulawesi and its satellite islands. The male is dark slaty-grey while the female is brownish with barring. It inhabits lowland and montane forests.

children.name: Edolisoma melas
children.description: An all-dark cicadabird found in the lowland forests of New Guinea and the Aru Islands. The male is glossy black while the female is dark brown. Its song is a rapid, insect-like trill.

children.name: Edolisoma salvadorii
children.description: A medium-sized cicadabird with a distinctive yellow eye, found in the montane forests of New Guinea. The male is slaty-grey and the female is brownish with barring.

children.name: Edolisoma holopolium
children.description: A large cicadabird found in the Solomon Islands, with the male slaty-grey and the female rufous with barring. It inhabits lowland and hill forests.

children.name: Edolisoma monacha
children.description: A striking cicadabird with a black hood, grey upperparts, and white underparts, endemic to the island of Halmahera in the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland forests.

children.name: Edolisoma insipidum
children.description: A plain grey cicadabird found in the lowland forests of New Guinea and the Bismarck Archipelago. Its song is a thin, insect-like trill.

children.name: Edolisoma remotum
children.description: A small cicadabird found in the Bismarck Archipelago and Solomon Islands, with the male dark grey and the female brownish with barring. It inhabits lowland forests.

children.name: Edolisoma nehrkorni
children.description: A small cicadabird endemic to the island of Waigeo off the coast of New Guinea. It inhabits lowland forests and is poorly known.

children.name: Edolisoma mindanense
children.description: A medium-sized cicadabird found in the Philippines, with the male dark grey above and pale below and the female brownish with barring. It inhabits lowland and montane forests.

children.name: Edolisoma grayi
children.description: A medium-sized cicadabird endemic to the island of New Britain in the Bismarck Archipelago. It inhabits lowland forests.

children.name: Edolisoma parkinsoni
children.description: A cicadabird found in the lowland forests of the Bismarck Archipelago and the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Edolisoma amboinense
children.description: A medium-sized cicadabird found in the lowland forests of the Moluccas.

children.name: Edolisoma dohertyi
children.description: A cicadabird found in the Lesser Sundas, inhabiting lowland forests.

children.name: Edolisoma timoriense
children.description: A medium-sized cicadabird endemic to the island of Timor in the Lesser Sundas.

children.name: Edolisoma obiense
children.description: A dark grey cicadabird endemic to the island of Obi in the Moluccas, inhabiting lowland forests.

children.name: Edolisoma sulaense
children.description: A small cicadabird endemic to the Sula Islands of the Moluccas, inhabiting lowland forests.

children.name: Lalage
children.description: The trillers and smaller cuckooshrikes — a genus of elegant, slender passerines with black, white, and grey plumage patterns, named for the male's distinctive reeling trilling call. They are more active and acrobatic than the larger Coracina cuckooshrikes, often flicking their wings rapidly. Found across Africa, Asia, Australia, and the Pacific islands.

children.name: Lalage poliocephala
children.description: A small, grey-headed cuckooshrike with a white belly and distinctive white wing-bars, endemic to the Philippine islands. It inhabits forests, forest edges, and secondary growth from lowlands to mid-elevations.

children.name: Lalage tricolor
children.description: A small, striking black-and-white cuckooshrike in which the breeding male is glossy black above with prominent white wing-patches and white underparts, found across Australia and the Lesser Sundas. It is named for its energetic, undulating display flight accompanied by a distinctive, rattling trill.

children.name: Lalage atrovirens
children.description: A small, dark-backed triller with a prominent black brow and white underparts, found in New Guinea and the surrounding islands. It inhabits lowland forests, forest edges, and secondary growth.

children.name: Lalage leucomela
children.description: A medium-sized triller with a black crown, white eyebrow, and variable underparts ranging from white to buff, found across New Guinea, the Bismarck Archipelago, and northern Australia. It inhabits mangroves, rainforest edges, and vine thickets.

children.name: Lalage sueurii
children.description: A small, striking triller with a black crown, white shoulder patches, and pale grey underparts, found across Indonesia and East Timor. It inhabits open woodlands, savannas, and cultivated areas.

children.name: Lalage melanoptera
children.description: A medium-sized cuckooshrike with a black head, grey upperparts, and white underparts, found across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It inhabits forests, woodlands, and well-wooded gardens.

children.name: Lalage leucopygialis
children.description: A small triller with a white rump, black upperparts, and white underparts, found in the lowland forests of Sulawesi and the Sula Islands. It inhabits forests and forest edges.

children.name: Lalage aurea
children.description: A colourful triller with a black head and upperparts, white wing bars, and a rich rufous belly, found in the lowland forests of the Moluccas. It forages actively in the canopy.

children.name: Lalage maculosa
children.description: A small triller with highly variable plumage across its island range, found in Fiji, Tonga, Samoa, and the Solomon Islands. It has a black crown, grey upperparts, and white underparts with variable barring.

children.name: Lalage sharpei
children.description: A small triller endemic to the islands of Upolu and Savaii in Samoa, with a black crown, grey upperparts, and white underparts. It inhabits forests and plantations.

children.name: Lalage nigra
children.description: A small, striking black-and-white triller found across Southeast Asia, the Philippines, and the Greater Sundas. It inhabits open woodlands, forests edges, and plantations.

children.name: Lalage moesta
children.description: A small triller with a white eyebrow, black crown, and grey upperparts, endemic to the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland forests and forages in the canopy.

children.name: Lalage fagei
children.description: A medium-sized triller with a black crown, grey upperparts, and pale underparts, found in the Bismarck Archipelago. It inhabits lowland forests.

children.name: Lalage typica
children.description: A small triller endemic to the Comoros Islands, with a black crown, grey upperparts, and white underparts. It inhabits forests and forest edges.

children.name: Pericrocotus
children.description: The minivets — a strikingly colourful genus of small passerines in the cuckooshrike family, with brilliant red, orange, or yellow underparts contrasting with black or grey upperparts. They are among the most brightly coloured of all Asian songbirds and are often seen in fast-moving flocks that sweep through the forest canopy in search of insects.

children.name: Pericrocotus speciosus
children.description: A brilliant red-and-black minivet found in the forests of the Himalayas, China, and Southeast Asia. The male has vivid red underparts and wing patches, while the female is yellow. It is often seen in noisy flocks.

children.name: Pericrocotus divaricatus
children.description: A grey-and-white minivet lacking the bright colours of other minivets, found across eastern and South Asia. It has a distinctive white collar and grey upperparts. It migrates south for winter.

children.name: Pericrocotus roseus
children.description: A small, pale minivet with rosy-pink underparts in the male and yellowish underparts in the female, found in the Himalayas and Southeast Asia. It inhabits deciduous and mixed forests.

children.name: Pericrocotus cinnamomeus
children.description: A small, grey-backed minivet with orange-cinnamon underparts in the male, found across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It inhabits forests, woodlands, and gardens.

children.name: Pericrocotus erythropygius
children.description: A striking black-and-white minivet with a red rump in the male, found in the dry forests of the Indian subcontinent. It inhabits open forests and scrub.

children.name: Pericrocotus igneus
children.description: A brilliant fiery-red minivet found in the lowland forests of the Philippines, Borneo, and Sumatra. The male has vivid red underparts contrasting with black upperparts. It is often seen in fast-moving flocks.

children.name: Pericrocotus solaris
children.description: A colourful minivet with a grey chin, yellow or red underparts, and black upperparts with white wing patches, found in the Himalayas and Southeast Asia. It inhabits montane forests.

children.name: Pericrocotus flammeus
children.description: A bright orange-and-black minivet found in the forests of India, Southeast Asia, and southern China. The male has brilliant orange underparts and the female is yellow. It is often seen in active flocks in the canopy.

children.name: Pericrocotus ethologus
children.description: A medium-sized minivet with a relatively long tail, red or yellow underparts, and black upperparts, found from Afghanistan to Southeast Asia. It inhabits montane and submontane forests.

children.name: Pericrocotus brevirostris
children.description: A medium-sized minivet with a relatively short, stout bill, red or yellow underparts, and grey upperparts, found in the montane forests of Southeast Asia.

children.name: Pericrocotus miniatus
children.description: A brilliant red-and-black minivet endemic to the mountains of Sumatra and Java. The male has vivid red underparts and the female is yellow. It inhabits montane forests.

children.name: Pericrocotus leucophaeus
children.description: A pale grey minivet with a white throat and red or yellow patches on the wings and tail, found from the Himalayas to Southeast Asia. It inhabits forests and woodlands.

children.name: Pericrocotus tegimae
children.description: A small minivet endemic to the Ryukyu Islands of Japan, with grey upperparts and red or yellow underparts. It inhabits forests and is the only minivet found in Japan.
